skill.name: python-typing-patterns
skill.description: Guidance for adding modern Python type hints, mypy and pyright configuration, overloads, protocols, and runtime validation patterns.
skill.summary: Apply practical Python typing patterns with static checker support.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical combinations, but review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration. Most findings are false positives from markdown code fences, typing examples, package names, and configuration snippets. The only executable component is a local shell helper that runs mypy and pyright, so publication is acceptable with a low-risk note.

low_findings.title: Local Type Checker Command Execution
low_findings.description: The shell helper invokes command -v, mypy, pyright, mktemp, and rm. This is expected for a type-checking utility, uses fixed tool names, and quotes the user-selected target path. It remains a low operational risk because running the script executes local developer tools and reads project files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and semantically match the script purpose. I did not find evidence of arbitrary command construction or network exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Network Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The network hits are example type signatures, a sample requests.get call, dependency names, and a pre-commit repository URL. They are not executed by the skill unless a user copies examples into their own project.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited network references are inside markdown or configuration examples. No skill runtime code sends data to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Crypto, Credential, And Reconnaissance Matches Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: Static hits for weak cryptography, certificate or key material, and reconnaissance map to typing terms such as key names, strict checker settings, ignored imports, or table entries. No cryptographic operation, credential file, or host reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain documentation and Python typing examples rather than secret access or cryptographic APIs. Adjacent context did not show malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged As Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are markdown code fences and inline code examples in reference material. These snippets explain typing patterns and are not executable during normal skill loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The repeated backtick detections align with fenced markdown blocks. The files are reference documents, not shell scripts.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Handling In Strict Pyright Mode
low_findings.description: The script creates a temporary pyright configuration with mktemp and removes it after pyright returns. This is legitimate local filesystem use, but interruption before cleanup could leave a small temporary config file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temporary file behavior is explicit and limited to a generated pyright config. It is not used to collect secrets or persist hidden data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Type Checker Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The shell helper invokes command -v, mypy, pyright, mktemp, and rm. This is expected for a type-checking utility, uses fixed tool names, and quotes the user-selected target path. It remains a low operational risk because running the script executes local developer tools and reads project files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly visible and semantically match the script purpose. I did not find evidence of arbitrary command construction or network exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Python projects often gain type hints unevenly, which leaves APIs hard to verify. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical patterns for annotations, checkers, protocols, overloads, and runtime validation.
actual_capabilities: Explains modern Python annotation syntax for functions, collections, optionals, and unions.
Provides patterns for TypeVar, Generic, Protocol, Callable, Literal, Final, and TypedDict.
Shows overload patterns for precise return types across input variants.
Includes mypy and pyright configuration examples for strict type checking.
Covers runtime validation options with Pydantic, typeguard, and beartype.
Includes a shell helper for running mypy and pyright on a selected project path.
limitations: Examples assume Python 3.10 or newer, with some patterns requiring Python 3.11.
The skill provides guidance and templates, not automatic migration of an entire codebase.
The shell helper depends on locally installed mypy or pyright commands.
Runtime validation examples require users to install and configure the chosen library.

use_cases.title: Type New Python Modules
use_cases.description: Add clear annotations and collection types while designing a new module API.

use_cases.title: Harden Existing Services
use_cases.description: Introduce mypy or pyright settings and improve weak annotations in service code.

use_cases.title: Document Library Interfaces
use_cases.description: Use protocols, overloads, and generics to make reusable library behavior easier to verify.

prompt_templates.title: Annotate a Function
prompt_templates.prompt: Add Python type hints to this function. Use modern Python 3.10 syntax and explain any optional values.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Type Checking
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a practical mypy and pyright configuration for this Python project. Start strict where it is safe and explain gradual adoption steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Generic APIs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this API and propose TypeVar, Generic, Protocol, or Callable patterns that improve type safety without overcomplicating usage.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Advanced Typing
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this typed Python module for overload correctness, type narrowing gaps, runtime validation needs, and checker configuration risks.

output_examples.input: A function accepts either text or bytes and returns the same kind of value.
output_examples.output: The response explains overload signatures, the combined implementation signature, and how callers receive precise return types.

output_examples.input: A project wants stricter static checking without breaking all legacy modules at once.
output_examples.output: The response suggests strict defaults, targeted overrides for tests or legacy modules, and a gradual migration plan.

output_examples.input: A service boundary receives dictionaries from external clients.
output_examples.output: The response compares TypedDict for static structure with Pydantic for runtime validation and error reporting.

best_practices: Prefer precise public API types before annotating every internal variable.
Use Protocol for structural behavior and TypedDict or Pydantic for shaped dictionaries.
Run mypy or pyright in CI and tighten configuration gradually for legacy code.
anti_patterns: Do not use Any to silence checker errors without a migration reason.
Do not add overloads unless each overload changes the accepted input or return type.
Do not rely on static type hints for untrusted runtime input without validation.

faq.question: Which Python versions does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets Python 3.10 and newer. Some examples use Python 3.11 features such as Self and TypeVarTuple.

faq.question: Does it support both mypy and pyright?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes configuration guidance and a helper script for running mypy, pyright, or both.

faq.question: Can this skill replace runtime validation?
faq.answer: No. Type hints help static analysis. Use Pydantic, typeguard, or beartype when data must be checked at runtime.

faq.question: Is it useful for legacy codebases?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include gradual typing patterns and scoped checker overrides for older modules.

faq.question: Does it include security-sensitive behavior?
faq.answer: It includes a local script that runs type checkers. The audit found no exfiltration or malicious network behavior.

faq.question: What should users provide in prompts?
faq.answer: Provide the Python version, checker choice, current annotations, and any runtime validation requirements.
